---
title: "Mathematical foundations of the horns index"
subtitle: "Complete derivations for $h$ and $h^*$"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: pdflatex
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
  - \usepackage{amssymb}
  - \usepackage{amsthm}
  - \usepackage{ifthen}
  - \theoremstyle{definition}
  - \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
  - \newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
  - \newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
  - \newtheorem{definition}{Definition}
---

TODO: SORT OUT USAGE OF S^2 AND SIGMA^2 IN THE ODD-N SECTION

\section{Introduction}

Here, we define the horns index, $h$, as a measure of dispersion for integer data. More precisely, $h$ is the ratio of the observed sample variance to the maximum possible sample variance.

We also define a special case, $h^*$. The difference is that $h$ accounts for finite sample constraints, whereas $h^*$ is solely based on the theoretical limit that follows from the range of the original scale. In this way, $h^*$ corresponds to an infinite sample.

\section{Basic concepts}

Let $k$ be the number of scale points, $n$ the sample size, $\bar{x}$ the arithmetic mean, $s^2$ the sample variance, and $\sigma^2$ the population variance.

\begin{definition}[Horns index]
$$h = \frac{s^2}{s^2_{\max}}$$
where $s^2_{\max}$ is the maximum attainable $s^2$ given $k$ and $n$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Infinite-$n$ horns index]
$$h^* = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2_{\max}}$$
where $\sigma^2_{\max}$ is the maximum attainable $\sigma^2$ given $k$ only.

The maximum variance formulas on which the definitions of $h$ and $h^*$ rely will be the subject of the remainder of this document. Note that they account for the parity of $n$, i.e., its property of being even or odd.

\end{definition}

\section{Maximum variance for finite samples}

\subsection{The geometric constraint}

\begin{theorem}[Maximum configuration]
Maximum variance is achieved by placing all observations at the scale extremes (positions 1 and $k$).
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Even $n$}: Place $\frac{n}{2}$ at position 1 and $\frac{n}{2}$ at position $k$
\item \textbf{Odd $n$}: Place $\frac{n - 1}{2}$ at position 1 and $\frac{n + 1}{2}$ at position $k$
\end{itemize}
\end{theorem}

\subsection{Calculating maximum variance}

\begin{theorem}[Maximum variance for even $n$]
For even $n$ with perfect split:
\begin{itemize}
\item Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{k + 1}{2}$
\item Sum of squared deviations: $SSD = n \cdot \frac{(k-1)^2}{4}$
\item Population variance: $\sigma^2_{\max} = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4}$
\item Sample variance: $s^2_{\max} = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \frac{n}{n-1}$
\end{itemize}
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
With $\frac{n}{2}$ observations at each extreme:

\begin{align}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\frac{n}{2} \cdot 1 + \frac{n}{2} \cdot k}{n} \\
  &= \frac{n + nk}{ 2n } \\
  &= \frac{k + 1}{2}
\end{align}


The sum of squared deviations:
\begin{align}
SSD &= \frac{n}{2}\left(1 - \frac{k+1}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{n}{2}\left(k - \frac{k+1}{2}\right)^2 \\
  &= \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{2 - (k+1)}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{2k - (k+1)}{2}\right)^2 \\
  &= \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{1-k}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{k-1}{2}\right)^2 \\
  &= 2 \ \frac{n}{2} \left(\frac{k-1}{2}\right)^2 \\
  &= \frac{n \ (k-1)^2}{4}
\end{align}

Therefore, with an even $n$, the maximal population variance $\sigma^2_{\max}$ and the maximal sample variance $s^2_{\max}$ are as follows:

\begin{align}
\sigma^2_{\max} &= \frac{SSD}{n} \\
  &= \frac{(k-1)^2}{4}
\end{align}

\begin{align}
s^2_{\max} &= \frac{SSD}{n-1} \\
  &= \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \frac{n}{n-1}
\end{align}

\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Maximum variance for odd $n$]
For odd $n$ with uneven split:
\begin{itemize}
\item Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{k+1}{2} + \frac{k-1}{2n}$
\item Sum of squared deviations: $SSD = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n^2}\right)$
\item Population variance: $\sigma^2_{\max} = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot (1 - \frac{1}{n^2})$
\item Sample variance: $s^2_{\max} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n^2}\right)$
\end{itemize}
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
With $\frac{n-1}{2}$ at position 1 and $\frac{n+1}{2}$ at position $k$:
\begin{align}
\bar{x} &= \frac{\frac{n-1}{2} + k \cdot \frac{n+1}{2}}{n} \\
  &= \frac{k+1}{2} + \frac{k-1}{2n}
\end{align}

As $1$ and $k$ are the only values at which any observations are located, we can express the deviations from the mean $\bar{x}$ as $d_1$ and $d_k$:
\begin{align}
d_1 &= 1 - \bar{x} \\
  &= -\frac{k-1}{2} \cdot \frac{n+1}{n}
\end{align}

\begin{align}
d_k &= k - \bar{x} \\
  &= \frac{k-1}{2} \cdot \frac{n-1}{n}
\end{align}

Sum of squared deviations:
$$SSD = \frac{n-1}{2} \cdot d_1^2 + \frac{n+1}{2} \cdot d_k^2$$

After algebra (expanding and simplifying):
$$SSD = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n^2}\right)$$

Therefore, with an odd $n$:
\begin{align}
\sigma^2_{\max} &= \frac{SSD}{n} \\
  &= \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n^2}\right)
\end{align}

\begin{align}
s^2_{\max} &= \frac{SSD}{n-1} \\
  &= \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n^2}\right)
\end{align}

\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Unified expression of $s^2_{\max}$] Theorems 2 and 3 can be combined into a single equation that holds for any $n$.
\

The formulas for $s^2_{\max}$ in Theorems 2 and 3 are only distinguished by the parity of $n$ (even or odd), such that the presence or absence of the odd-$n$ correction factor $1 - \frac{1}{n^2}$ constitutes the only difference between them. To emphasize the commonalities between the two equations and reduce them to a single, consistent representation that holds for any value of $n$, irrespective of parity, a unified equation for $s^2_{\max}$ shall be provided below.

First, note that $(-1)^n$ equals 1 if $n$ is even, and $-1$ if $n$ is odd. Therefore:

$$
\frac{1- (-1)^n}{2} = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } n \text{ is even} \\
1 & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}
\end{cases}
$$

We can use this term to modify the odd-$n$ correction factor in a way that renders it inert iff $n$ is even. We thus obtain a generalized parity correction that is neutral for even $n$ but otherwise has the desired correcting behavior for the odd-$n$ case:

\begin{align}
1 - \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot \frac{1- (-1)^n}{2} &= 1 - \frac{1- (-1)^n}{2 n^2} \\
  &=\frac{2 n^2 - 1 + (-1)^n}{2 n^2} \\
  &= \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } n \text{ is even} \\
1 - \frac{1}{n^2} & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}
\end{cases}
\end{align}

Consequently, using this generalized expression instead of the one from Theorem 3 (that is specific to the odd-$n$ case) yields the desired general formula for the sample variance that applies to any $n$:

$$
s^2_{\max} = \frac{(k-1)^2}{4} \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{2 n^2 - 1 + (-1)^n}{2 n^2}
$$

\end{theorem}

\section{The relationship between $h$ and $h^*$}

\begin{proposition}[Convergence]
As $n \to \infty$:
\begin{enumerate}
\item The parity correction converges to 1, so the odd-even distinction vanishes: $\frac{2 n^2 - 1 + (-1)^n}{2 n^2} \to 1$
\item Bessel's correction also converges to 1, so the sample-population distinction vanishes: $\frac{n}{n - 1} \to 1$
\item Therefore, the horns index converges to the infinite-$n$ horns index: $h \to h^*$
\end{enumerate}
\end{proposition}
